worth_it_explanation: Viewability confirms that an ad had a chance to be seen; attention signals go further by estimating whether an ad was likely noticed and engaged with. IAS Quality Attention combines viewability, time-in-view, and engagement signals into a single score that distinguishes high-attention placements from technically viewable ones that users scroll past. These checks confirm whether the account is measuring attention at all, and whether the data it produces is feeding back into buying decisions. If neither is true, the measurement spend produces no change in outcomes.
intro: Integral Ad Science (IAS) Quality Attention is a metric that layers additional signals on top of binary viewability to estimate whether an ad had a genuine opportunity to register with a user. Where viewability records whether a qualifying percentage of pixels was on screen for a minimum duration, attention scoring incorporates factors such as time-in-view, position on page, scroll velocity, and device interaction signals to produce a score that reflects the quality of the exposure. These checks confirm two things: that the account is collecting attention data rather than relying on viewability alone, and that the data is used to inform supply and creative decisions rather than sitting in a report that no one reads.

audit_checks.title: The account optimizes on viewability alone and does not use IAS Quality Attention scores
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Viewability confirms a minimum exposure threshold was met. It does not distinguish a placement where a user scrolled past in under a second from one where a user paused and read. Optimizing on viewability alone treats those two placements identically, so the account cannot steer budget toward supply that produces higher-quality exposures.

educate.base:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) offers a metric called Quality Attention that combines viewability with additional exposure signals, including time-in-view, on-screen position, scroll behavior, and device interaction patterns, to produce a score for each ad placement. The score is designed to rank placements by the likelihood that a user actually processed the ad rather than technically meeting the minimum viewability threshold.

The MRC viewability standard, which defines a display ad as viewable when at least 50 percent of pixels are on screen for one continuous second, was designed as a floor, not a quality signal. A placement that meets that standard by the smallest possible margin, 50 percent visible for exactly one second, scores the same as a placement that was fully visible for fifteen seconds. Quality Attention scoring separates those two scenarios.

It is worth noting that attention measurement is newer than viewability measurement and does not yet have a single agreed industry standard. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) and the Media Rating Council (MRC) released joint attention measurement guidelines in November 2025 that describe four categories of measurement approaches, including data signal-based methods of the type IAS Quality Attention uses. IAS's specific methodology is proprietary, so its scores cannot be directly compared to attention scores from other vendors. You may want to treat Quality Attention scores as a relative ranking signal within IAS-measured inventory rather than as an absolute cross-vendor benchmark.

A few signs this check applies:
  • IAS is active on the account for viewability verification, but the IAS Signal reporting interface shows no Quality Attention data for any campaign
  • The account's optimization strategy references viewability rate as the primary supply-quality signal, with no mention of attention scores in insertion order (IO) or line item (LI) targeting or reporting
  • The media plan uses viewability thresholds as the sole supply-quality filter when evaluating publisher performance, with no attention tier or score range included
You may want to confirm whether Quality Attention scoring is enabled in IAS Signal for the campaigns under review before drawing conclusions from viewability data alone.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running display and video campaigns to drive awareness before a promotional period, the difference between a technically viewable placement and a genuinely attended one is the difference between an impression that built recall and one that did not. You may want to confirm whether Quality Attention scores are available in IAS Signal and whether they are used to filter or weight supply decisions alongside viewability.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running upper-funnel display to build awareness before a demand-capture phase, attention quality on the awareness flight affects how primed the retargeting audience actually is. You may want to confirm whether Quality Attention data is collected on awareness campaigns and whether it informs which placements are retained in the media plan for the next flight.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Quality Attention methodology and Signal reporting documentation is available in the IAS Signal help center, which requires an active IAS account login. Your IAS account team can provide setup guides and methodology details on request.

investigate.intro: Open IAS Signal and confirm whether Quality Attention data appears for the campaigns under review. Check whether attention scores appear in any active targeting or optimization rules.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the reporting section for the advertiser or campaign under review.

Check whether a Quality Attention column or dimension is available in the standard reporting view. If the column is not present, confirm with the IAS account team whether Quality Attention scoring is enabled for this account.

If Quality Attention data is present, note the score distribution across placements. Look for whether any placements show high viewability rates but low attention scores, which would indicate technically viewable inventory that users are not engaging with.

Review the campaign's IO and LI targeting settings in the demand-side platform (DSP) the account uses. Confirm whether any attention score threshold or attention tier is included as a supply filter alongside viewability targets.
Ask the media team whether Quality Attention data from IAS Signal is part of the standard reporting package shared with stakeholders, or whether reporting uses only viewability metrics.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether Quality Attention data is available and active in IAS Signal for the campaigns reviewed, and whether attention scores appear in any targeting or optimization rules. A campaign with Quality Attention enabled and scores reflected in at least one supply filter or reporting template passes. A campaign where IAS is active for viewability only, with no Quality Attention data collected or referenced in optimization, is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the viewability-only signals currently in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether IAS Quality Attention is enabled in Signal and whether attention scores appear in any targeting or optimization settings above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS setup measures viewability but does not collect or apply IAS Quality Attention scores. Viewability confirms a minimum exposure threshold was met; it does not distinguish placements where users paused and engaged from those they scrolled past after a single second. Without attention data, the account cannot identify which supply sources produce high-quality exposures or steer budget toward them. {{voice_recommend}} confirming with the IAS account team whether Quality Attention scoring is enabled, enabling it for active campaigns if it is not, and incorporating attention scores alongside viewability thresholds in supply evaluation.

audit_checks.title: IAS Quality Attention data is collected but not used to inform supply or creative decisions
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Measurement that produces no change in buying or creative decisions adds cost without benefit. If Quality Attention scores appear in IAS Signal but the account continues optimizing on viewability thresholds alone, the attention data is decorative rather than functional.

educate.base:
Collecting attention data and acting on it are two different things. An account can have IAS Quality Attention enabled, see scores in IAS Signal reports, and still make no use of those scores when evaluating publishers, adjusting bids, or briefing creative. When that happens, the measurement produces no change in outcomes.

Acting on attention data takes two forms. The first is supply optimization: using Quality Attention scores to identify placements that score consistently high and to deprioritize or exclude placements that score consistently low despite meeting viewability thresholds. The second is creative feedback: comparing Quality Attention scores across creative formats or executions to understand whether format, size, or message affects how attended the ads are.

Neither use requires a full workflow change. Supply feedback can be as simple as a monthly review of Quality Attention score by publisher or placement in IAS Signal, with low-scoring placements flagged for review with the DSP team. Creative feedback can be a cross-campaign comparison of attention scores by ad format in the same report view.

A few signs this check applies:
  • Quality Attention scores appear in IAS Signal for the account, but the media team cannot confirm the last time attention data was used to make a supply or creative decision
  • Publisher performance evaluations in the account use viewability rate and cost per thousand impressions (CPM) as the primary signals, with attention scores absent from the scorecard
  • Creative rotation decisions reference click-through rate or completion rate but do not include attention score comparisons across executions
You may want to confirm with the media team how Quality Attention data flows from IAS Signal into the regular performance review cycle before concluding whether the data is being used.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer whose media team reviews publisher performance on a monthly or quarterly basis, adding a Quality Attention score column to the standard publisher scorecard is a straightforward way to ensure the data informs decisions. You may want to confirm whether the current scorecard includes attention data alongside viewability and CPM.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser evaluating supply sources across awareness campaigns, attention score by placement tells you which inventory produced exposures likely to prime users for downstream demand capture. You may want to confirm whether placement-level attention scores from IAS Signal are reviewed when renewing or dropping supply sources.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal documentation on interpreting Quality Attention scores and building attention-based reporting views is available in the IAS Signal help center, which requires an active IAS account login. Your IAS account team can walk through standard reporting templates on request.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether Quality Attention data from IAS Signal is referenced in publisher evaluations, targeting decisions, or creative reviews for the campaigns under review.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and pull a placement-level or publisher-level report for the campaigns under review. Confirm whether the report includes a Quality Attention score column.

Sort the report by Quality Attention score and identify any placements that score in the bottom quartile despite meeting the account's viewability threshold. Note whether those placements are still active in the current media plan.
Ask the media team to share the publisher scorecard or performance review document used for the most recent optimization cycle. Confirm whether Quality Attention scores appear in it.

In the DSP the account uses, check whether any IO or LI targeting rules reference attention thresholds from IAS. If IAS provides a direct data feed or integration to the DSP, confirm whether attention data is flowing through that integration.
Review the most recent creative report or rotation log. Confirm whether attention scores by ad format or execution are included in the comparison criteria used to rotate or retire creatives.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether Quality Attention score data from IAS Signal appears in publisher evaluations, DSP targeting rules, or creative performance reviews for the period under audit. An account where attention scores are referenced in at least one of those three decision points passes. An account where attention data is collected but absent from all three decision points is a fail, recorded with the report or scorecard reviewed and the signals currently in use in its place.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether Quality Attention scores appear in publisher evaluations, DSP targeting rules, or creative performance reviews above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} collects IAS Quality Attention data but does not use it to inform supply evaluation, targeting, or creative decisions. Measurement that does not change buying or creative behavior adds cost without benefit. Publisher scorecards and optimization cycles reference viewability rate and CPM, while attention scores sit unused in IAS Signal reports. {{voice_recommend}} incorporating Quality Attention scores into the standard publisher scorecard, reviewing placement-level attention data at the same cadence as viewability and delivery data, and including attention score comparisons in creative rotation reviews so the data informs decisions rather than documenting them.
